mappings.rationaleAI: Systematic investigations of human anatomy in notebooks exemplify the Renaissance influence of artists' illustrations on biological knowledge and the scientific study of anatomy through dissection (A.1.d).
Broad history of science tracing evolution, germ theory, and genetics provides overarching context for the origin and development of biological ideas (A, A.1).
Medieval period including Islamic expansion and scholasticism corresponds to biology in the Middle Ages with Arabian biologists and the development of botany and zoology (A.1.c).
Zoology rooted in Aristotle's naturalism connects to Greco-Roman theories of classification (A.1.b); its formalization as a separate discipline in the Middle Ages (A.1.c).

Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection is the foundational framework of the life sciences; while the outline only covers through the Renaissance, Darwin's work is central to the broader history of biological sciences (A.1).
Islamic Golden Age contributions to biology, medicine, and natural sciences correspond to the influence of Arabian biologists in the Middle Ages (A.1.c).
Homo sapiens anatomy and biological characteristics connect to Greco-Roman theories about mankind, anatomical discoveries, and classification (A.1.b).
Evolution as the change in heritable characteristics of biological populations is central to the history of biological sciences broadly, though the detailed outline ends at the Renaissance (A.1).
Asexual and sexual reproduction strategies connect to Aristotelian concepts of reproduction and heredity in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b).
Aristotle's empirical taxonomy and concepts of classification, reproduction, heredity, and descent are foundational to biology in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b).
The European movable-type printing press directly relates to the influence of the craft of printing on the dissemination of botanical knowledge during the Renaissance (A.1.d).
Ancient Greek humoral theory and medieval medical institutions connect to Greco-Roman biological and anatomical ideas (A.1.b) and biology in the Middle Ages (A.1.c).
Egyptian medicine and biological knowledge correspond to views of life in ancient Eastern civilizations (A.1.a).
Greek natural science, philosophy, and mathematics connect to biology in the Greco-Roman world including Aristotelian classification and anatomical discoveries (A.1.b).
Vesalius's anatomical revolution and the beginning of scientific study of anatomy through dissection connect to biology in the Renaissance (A.1.d).
The 3.7-billion-year history of life from microbial origins to present provides the subject matter for the entire history of biological sciences (A.1).
Biological inheritance and transmission of genetic information connect to Aristotelian concepts of heredity and descent in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b).
Biology as the scientific study of life spanning molecular to biosphere levels provides the overall framework for the history of the biological sciences (A, A.1).
Animal classification history from Aristotle through Linnaeus connects to Greco-Roman theories of classification and descent (A.1.b).
Pasteur's disproval of spontaneous generation and establishment of germ theory are pivotal events in the history of biological sciences (A.1).
Theories about the origin of life from non-living chemistry connect to Greco-Roman theories about mankind and the origin of life (A.1.b).
The concept of an individual living entity characterized by reproduction, growth, and metabolism connects to Aristotelian biological concepts in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b).
Scientific study of past life through fossils provides evidence for the history of biological ideas, connecting to the development of biological sciences (A.1).
Species as the foundational unit of biological classification connects to Aristotelian concepts of classification in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b).

Greek botanical investigations (A.1.b); botany developing as a separate discipline in the Middle Ages (A.1.c); printing and artists' illustrations disseminating botanical knowledge in the Renaissance (A.1.d).

Initial anatomical discoveries in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.b); further discoveries in the Middle Ages (A.1.c); beginning of scientific study of anatomy through dissection in the Renaissance (A.1.d).
Biochemistry as the study of chemical processes in living organisms is central to the history of biological sciences (A.1).
The craft of printing and artists' illustrations directly influenced the dissemination of botanical knowledge in the Renaissance (A.1.d).
Ecology as the study of relationships among organisms and environments is a key development within the biological sciences (A.1).
Ancient civilizations in West Asia, Africa, and East Asia correspond to views of life in ancient Eastern and Middle Eastern civilizations (A.1.a).
Mesopotamian civilization as a cradle of early knowledge connects to views of life in ancient Eastern civilizations (A.1.a).
The Renaissance influence of printing and artists' illustrations on dissemination of botanical knowledge and the beginning of scientific anatomy (A.1.d).
